description.s: Hob should be the obvious choice for any user-programming within Hobson. There should be no reason to choose JS except for familiarity. Hob — and the way it integrates with Hobson — should be better than JS for most things, and as good for the rest.

This is not "Hob is a nice option" or "Hob works for simple views." It's a stronger claim: a Hobson user who knows both JS and Hob should reach for Hob every time, because Hob understands Hobson in ways that JS never can.
Why Hob can be better than JS
Hob's advantage is not that it's a better general-purpose language — it isn't, and doesn't need to be. Its advantage is that it's a
language. Hobson concepts are Hob concepts:
is a reference to an item, not a string constant you look up.
are language-level operations, not API calls.
With JSON AST as canonical representation (see
), Hob code is stored in the same format as everything else in Hobson. It's queryable, transformable, and inspectable with the same tools as any item property.
Because Hob code runs through an evaluator (not
), the system can sandbox it, track its dependencies, inspect its state, and assist meaningfully. JS is opaque; Hob is transparent.
Users can define new syntactic patterns that operate on code structure. This is impossible in JS without a build system.
Enabled by code-as-data. Edit code structure directly, no syntax errors possible. The editor understands what you're editing because the code is structured data, not text.
is the escape hatch, not the goal
provides general JS interop — call any JS function, access any API. It's necessary but should feel like reaching for a screwdriver when the toolbox is right there. If a Hob view is mostly
calls, that's a signal that Hob is missing a primitive, not that the view is inherently JS-native.
Domain-specific ops are the ergonomic layer
Every pattern that recurs across Hobson programming should eventually have a Hob-native expression. The progression:
Promotion: add a Hob primitive that expresses the concept directly
, etc. were born. The same process continues for interactive/spatial concepts.
Primitives should be better, not just equivalent
When adding a new capability, don't just match what JS can do — make it better:
should be inspectable and auto-cleaned-up, not just mutable variables
should be declarative with automatic lifecycle management, not manual mount/unmount
should be lifecycle-aware with automatic cleanup, not manual bookkeeping
(eventually) should be language-level dependency tracking, not framework boilerplate

is not an optimization — it's the foundation for everything that makes Hob categorically better than JS. Queryable code, structural editing, safe macro expansion, and deep tooling all depend on code being stored as structured data.
19 views converted to Hob, including the spatial-canvas-view (most complex view in the system — 5 JS libraries, nested render-item, selector-filtered reactivity)
JSON AST is the canonical code representation — all Hob items stored as compact JSON, evaluator dispatches directly on JSON types
Persistent view state via atoms + get-view-config/update-view-config!
Sub-item rendering with options map (view, sibling-container, on-cycle, decorator, navigate-to)
Document-level events with automatic cleanup (on-document!, on-cleanup!)
— views declare what slice of data they care about, re-renders fire only when that slice changes
Selector pins — explicit selectors can't be widened by later bare get-item calls
Morphdom handler retention — old event handlers kept (correct DOM refs) instead of transferred from discarded tree
Domain ops cover the common cases for item-centric programming
Macro system works and is used in practice
More view conversions (test-suite, hobson-instance, type-workshop are ready now)
Structural editor phase 7 (copy/paste, comment support, context-sensitive hints)
Reactive transclusion (static transclusion works; making it reactive/editable is the next leap)
Making Hob the default language for new items
When evaluating any change to Hob, ask:
does this make Hob more obviously the right choice for Hobson programming?
Adding a primitive? It should make the Hob version better than the JS version, not just possible.
Designing an API? Express Hobson concepts directly, don't just wrap JS APIs.
Building tooling? Leverage code-as-data — do things JS tooling can't.
Converting a view? If it's mostly
, pause and build the missing primitive first.
